title: The Day of Jezreel
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 02-16-25 -PM- The Day of Jezreel - Hosea 1_10-2_1 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Hinge from Judgment to Hope
subpoints: Hosea 1:9 ended with the verdict: 'You are not My people, and I am not your God'
Verses 10-2:1 form an immediate reversal: 'Yet...' — one of the most important words in Scripture
God's faithfulness to the Abrahamic covenant (Gen. 22:17) is the basis for the reversal: Israel's unfaithfulness cannot annul God's promises
Pattern from 2 Chronicles 7:12-14: judgment is conditional on repentance — mercy is always available

outline.title: 1. The Scope of Mercy: Sons of the Living God
subpoints: 'In the place where it was said to them, You are not My people, there it shall be said to them, You are sons of the living God' (Hosea 1:10)
Paul quotes this text in Romans 9:25-26 as the basis for Gentile inclusion — the promise is broader than ethnic Israel
'Sons of the living God' — adoption language; God becomes Father to those who were foreigners to His covenant
Westminster Shorter Catechism Q.74: adoption is the act of God by which He takes those who were strangers and enemies and makes them His children with all the privileges thereof

outline.title: 2. The Reunification: One Head
subpoints: 'The children of Judah and the children of Israel shall be gathered together, and appoint for themselves one head' (Hosea 1:11)
Hosea 3:5 identifies this head: 'they shall seek the LORD their God and David their king' — the coming Davidic Messiah
The divided kingdom (since 931 BC) will be reunited under Christ — the one King of both Jew and Gentile
This is the eschatological vision: one flock, one shepherd (John 10:16)

outline.title: 3. The Exodus Imagery: Come Up from the Land
subpoints: 'They shall come up out of the land' (Hosea 1:11) — deliberate echo of Israel's Exodus from Egypt
Exodus 1:7 uses the same language of multiplying and filling the land — God is promising a new Exodus
Ezekiel 36:24-28: a new Exodus in which God gathers His people, gives them a new heart, and puts His Spirit within them
'For great will be the day of Jezreel' — the reversal of the curse into the fulfillment of the promise

outline.title: 4. The Reversal of the Three Names
subpoints: Hosea 2:1: 'Say to your brethren, My people, and to your sisters, Mercy is shown'
Jezreel: originally meant 'God scatters' (judgment); now reversed — God will sow (plant, restore, multiply)
Lo-Ruhamah ('no mercy shown') becomes Ruhamah ('mercy is shown') — God's compassion restored
Lo-Ammi ('not My people') becomes Ammi ('My people') — the covenant relationship restored
The Day of Jezreel = the day God sows His people back into the land; the curse becomes the blessing

outline.title: Application: The Joy of Adoption Marks Our Speech
subpoints: 'Say to your brethren, My people' — the reversal is to be announced; it shapes how God's people address one another
If you are in Christ, your identity has been reversed: you were Lo-Ammi, you are now Ammi; you were Lo-Ruhamah, you are now Ruhamah
The gospel of adoption should mark the everyday speech of God's people to one another — not just proclamation outward but declaration inward
This is the ultimate ground of Christian community: we are all children of the living God through the one Head, Jesus Christ

summary: Pastor Tito Lyro preaches on Hosea 1:10-2:1, the sudden turn from the devastating judgment of chapter 1 into a passage of radical hope. The single word 'yet' pivots the text: despite all Israel's unfaithfulness, God remains faithful to His Abrahamic covenant. The passage promises that those called 'not My people' will be called 'sons of the living God' — a promise Paul applies directly to Gentile inclusion in Romans 9:25-26. The divided kingdom will be gathered under one head (the Davidic Messiah, Hosea 3:5). An Exodus-like deliverance is promised. And all three symbolic names are reversed: Jezreel (God scatters → God sows), Lo-Ruhamah (no mercy → mercy shown), Lo-Ammi (not My people → My people). The Day of Jezreel is the day God's planting bears fruit — and the application is that this reversal should shape how God's people address one another daily.
transcript:
## Introduction: The 'Yet' That Changes Everything

We ended last week at one of the lowest points in all of Scripture. Hosea 1:9:

> "Then God said: 'Call his name Lo-Ammi, for you are not My people, and I am not your God.'"

Three children. Three names. Jezreel — God scatters. Lo-Ruhamah — no mercy. Lo-Ammi — not My people. It is a devastating pronouncement. The covenant formula that has defined Israel's relationship to Yahweh since Sinai — *I will be your God and you will be My people* — is formally reversed.

And then — verse 10 begins: *Yet...*

One of the most important words in the Bible. It signals that God has not finished speaking. His last word is not judgment. His last word is mercy — because His mercy is rooted not in what Israel deserves, but in what He promised.

2 Chronicles 7:12-14 sets the pattern:

> "The LORD appeared to Solomon by night and said to him: 'I have heard your prayer and have chosen this place for Myself as a house of sacrifice. If I shut up heaven so that there is no rain, or if I command the locusts to devour the land, or if I send pestilence among My people; if My people who are called by My name will humble themselves, and pray and seek My face, and turn from their wicked ways, then I will hear from heaven and will forgive their sin and heal their land.'"

Judgment is real. It is not empty. But the door is never permanently closed. God's faithfulness to the Abrahamic covenant — *your descendants will be as the sand of the sea* (Gen. 22:17) — means that Israel's unfaithfulness cannot annul His promise.

## 1. Sons of the Living God

Verse 10:

> "Yet the number of the children of Israel shall be as the sand of the sea, which cannot be measured or numbered. And it shall come to pass in the place where it was said to them, 'You are not My people,' there it shall be said to them, 'You are sons of the living God.'"

This is adoption language. Where the verdict was 'You are not My people,' the reversal is not merely 'You are My people again' — it is *sons of the living God.* The covenant is not just restored; it is elevated.

Paul seizes on this text in Romans 9:25-26. He has been arguing that God's word has not failed — that the promise to Israel has not collapsed. And then he quotes Hosea:

> "As He says also in Hosea: 'I will call them My people, who were not My people, and her beloved, who was not beloved. And it shall come to pass in the place where it was said to them, You are not My people, there they shall be called sons of the living God.'"

Paul is not spiritualizing or distorting Hosea. He is reading Hosea accurately: the promise was always bigger than ethnic Israel. The grace that reverses Lo-Ammi into Ammi is the same grace that brings Gentiles — those who were *never* His people — into sonship through Christ.

The Westminster Shorter Catechism, Question 74, defines adoption: adoption is the act of God by which He receives those who were strangers and enemies into the number of His children, with all the privileges and blessings that belong to that relationship. Sons have access to the Father. Sons receive an inheritance. Sons bear the family name.

You were Lo-Ammi. In Christ, you are a son of the living God.

## 2. One Head

Verse 11:

> "Then the children of Judah and the children of Israel shall be gathered together, and appoint for themselves one head; and they shall come up out of the land, for great will be the day of Jezreel."

Since the division of the kingdom in 931 BC, Israel and Judah have been two kingdoms — rival, often hostile. The prophets dream of their reunification. Hosea says that reunification will happen: gathered together, one head.

Hosea 3:5 identifies who that head is: *They shall seek the LORD their God and David their king.* The coming Davidic king — the Messiah — will be the one head under whom the divided people of God are brought together. This is why Jesus says in John 10:16: *I have other sheep that are not of this fold. I must bring them also, and they will listen to My voice. So there will be one flock, one shepherd.*

The unification is not political — it is covenantal. Jew and Gentile, together, under the one King.

## 3. Come Up Out of the Land

'They shall come up out of the land' — this phrase is thick with Exodus resonance. Exodus 1:7 uses nearly identical language to describe the multiplication of Israel in Egypt before the Exodus: *the children of Israel were fruitful and increased abundantly, multiplied and grew exceedingly mighty; and the land was filled with them.*

Hosea is pointing to a new Exodus. Just as God brought Israel out of Egypt — out of a land of bondage and shame — He will bring them out again. Ezekiel 36:24-28 fills in what this new Exodus looks like:

> "I will take you from among the nations, gather you out of all countries, and bring you into your own land. Then I will sprinkle clean water on you, and you shall be clean... I will give you a new heart... I will put My Spirit within you and cause you to walk in My statutes."

The new Exodus is not a return to Canaan — it is the transformation of the heart by the Spirit of God.

## 4. The Day of Jezreel — The Reversal

'For great will be the day of Jezreel.' Now Hosea 2:1:

> "Say to your brethren, 'My people,' and to your sisters, 'Mercy is shown.'"

Look at what has happened to the three names:

**Jezreel** was introduced as the site of judgment — the place where the blood of Ahab's house was shed, the curse embedded in geography. But Jezreel literally means *God sows* — God plants. What was a monument to scattering becomes a monument to planting. The Day of Jezreel is not the day God scatters; it is the day God sows His people back into the land with abundance.

**Lo-Ruhamah** — 'no mercy shown' — becomes **Ruhamah** — 'mercy is shown.' The womb of God's compassion that had closed (that is the Hebrew root: *racham*, womb-love) is opened again. The mother who turned away her face turns back and embraces.

**Lo-Ammi** — 'not My people' — becomes **Ammi** — 'My people.' The covenant formula is restored: *I will be your God and you will be My people.* But more: you are not just people, you are *sons of the living God.*

This is the movement of the entire Bible. Exile — return. Death — resurrection. Rejection — adoption. Condemnation — justification. *Lo* is removed from every name.

## Application: The Gospel Marks Our Speech

Verse 2:1 ends with a word about speech: *Say to your brethren, My people, and to your sisters, Mercy is shown.*

The reversal is not just something that happens to you. It is something you are to announce to one another. The children of God are to speak to one another in the vocabulary of what God has done. Call your brothers *My people.* Call your sisters *Mercy is shown.*

This is what it looks like for the gospel to shape ordinary Christian community. Not just proclamation to outsiders — declaration among insiders. When you gather with God's people, you are gathering with those who were Lo-Ammi and are now Ammi. Those who were Lo-Ruhamah and are now Ruhamah. That reality should press into how you greet one another, how you pray for one another, how you speak of one another.

Romans 8:15: *You received the Spirit of adoption by whom we cry out, 'Abba, Father.'* Galatians 4:5: *To redeem those who were under the law, that we might receive the adoption as sons.* Ephesians 1:5: *He predestined us to adoption as sons through Jesus Christ.*

You are a son of the living God. Say so. Speak that way — to yourself and to your brothers and sisters in Christ. The Day of Jezreel has come.
